name: AML Alert Triage Pattern
description: Anti-Money Laundering (AML) alert triage is the first line of defense in a financial institution's BSA/AML program. Alerts must be processed within regulatory timeframes — and the consequence of misclassification in either direction carries asymmetric risk: failure to escalate a genuine SAR candidate is a regulatory violation; over-escalating creates analyst overload and desensitization. This pattern implements risk-score-based routing with hard rules that cannot be overridden by any agent action. The critical constraint: no AML alert can be auto-closed without human review — the human in the loop is non-negotiable in this domain.
trigger: AML monitoring system generates a new alert for any customer account or transaction.
logic: 1. Receive AML alert with risk score (0-100) from monitoring system. 2. Route by score tier: Score > 80 → immediate SAR consideration queue, compliance officer notified within 1h, Level 2 analyst assigned, hold any pending transactions on the flagged account pending review. Score 50-80 → analyst review queue, 4h SLA, Level 1 analyst assigned. Score < 50 → bulk review queue, 24h SLA, analyst batch assignment. 3. For all tiers: generate alert package — customer ID (anonymized for routing), transaction pattern summary, risk score breakdown, similar historical alerts on same customer. 4. Include: recommended review path (SAR consideration, EDD review, or standard review), not verdict. 5. Log: alert ID, score, tier assigned, routing timestamp, analyst assigned. 6. Never auto-close any alert. Never include PII in routing notifications.
escalation: Score > 80 and no analyst assignment within 1h: BSA Officer notified. 30-day SAR deadline tracking begins at alert creation. If SAR deadline at risk: BSA Officer + Compliance Director. Any transaction freeze applied: Legal notified.
ground_truth_check: Compliance Officer quarterly review: was routing tier accurate? Were SAR-candidate alerts correctly identified? False negative rate (genuine SAR that was in < 50 tier) is the critical metric.
promotion_criteria: 500+ alerts triaged. False negative rate < 5% (SAR candidates in wrong tier). SAR filing timeline compliance 100%. BSA Officer sign-off on triage accuracy. CAiO review.

name: Regulatory Report Generation
description: BSA/AML regulatory reports (SARs, CTRs) have precise format requirements, legally mandated content, and submission deadlines. Generating these reports from structured data reduces the error rate on required fields and ensures completeness — but they cannot be submitted without Compliance Officer sign-off. The pattern generates a high-quality draft; the human provides the final judgment. The confidence flagging for uncertain fields is the key feature: it focuses the reviewer's attention on the fields that need it, not the entire document.
trigger: Analyst has completed review and determined a SAR should be filed, OR CTR threshold transaction detected (currency transaction > $10,000 per FinCEN rules), OR other mandatory regulatory report trigger identified.
logic: 1. Retrieve transaction data and customer data for the report subject. 2. Pre-populate required fields from structured data: customer identification, transaction details, account information, dates. 3. For each field: calculate confidence score based on data completeness and source reliability. 4. Flag any field with confidence < 90% with: [REVIEW REQUIRED] marker, reason for low confidence, available data that informed the partial fill. 5. Generate narrative section draft (for SAR): what activity, why suspicious, what investigation was conducted. Draft uses plain language, no legal conclusions. 6. Package complete draft with cover sheet: flagged fields list, data sources used, recommended additional information to gather. 7. Route to Compliance Officer for review and sign-off. 8. Track submission deadline in monitoring queue.
escalation: Compliance Officer must sign off before any submission. If submission deadline is < 5 days and draft not yet reviewed: immediate escalation to Compliance Director. No draft is submitted under any circumstance without explicit sign-off.
ground_truth_check: Compliance Officer confirms: draft quality reduced review time vs. manual preparation. Flagged fields were correctly identified. Narrative draft required only minor revision. Filed report was accepted by FinCEN/regulator.
promotion_criteria: 50+ regulatory reports drafted. 100% submission rate with Compliance Officer sign-off. Zero reports submitted without sign-off. Compliance Officer reports draft quality reduces review time by > 30%. CAiO review.

name: Credit Decision Boundary
description: Credit limit decisions carry direct financial risk — both from under-limit (missed revenue, customer churn) and over-limit (credit loss, concentration risk). This pattern draws the decision boundary: small adjustments within established parameters are safe for automation; large adjustments or customers with impaired payment history require analyst review. The dual approval requirement for high-risk customers is not a constraint on speed — it is risk management proportional to exposure.
trigger: System proposes a credit limit change for a customer, OR credit review cycle is triggered for a customer account.
logic: 1. Calculate proposed change as percentage of current limit: (proposed - current) / current. 2. Check payment history: count missed payments in last 12 months. 3. Apply decision rules: Rule A — change > 20% in either direction → credit analyst review required (MEDIUM authority). Rule B — change <= 20% AND customer has <= 3 missed payments in 12 months AND within established risk parameters → auto-approve with Process Owner notification (LOW authority). Rule C — any limit change for customer with > 3 missed payments in 12 months → dual approval required: Credit Analyst + Credit Manager (HIGH authority). 4. For auto-approved changes (Rule B): notify Process Owner, log full decision rationale with data inputs. 5. For analyst review (Rule A): generate decision package with financial rationale, risk score, comparable customers. 6. For dual approval (Rule C): generate enhanced package with payment history, risk concentration, recommended limit with rationale.
escalation: Credit Analyst (Rule A and C). Credit Manager (Rule C dual approval). If credit limit change > $1M: also notify Chief Credit Officer.
ground_truth_check: 90-day post-decision review: did the approved limit change result in expected behavior (limit utilization, payment performance)? Adverse outcomes against auto-approved decisions are false-done candidates.
promotion_criteria: At maturity 2 (mechanical). Promotion to 3 requires: 100+ decisions processed, loss rate on auto-approved decisions comparable to analyst-reviewed decisions, Credit Manager validation of rule accuracy. CAiO review.

name: Audit Trail Mandatory
description: In financial services, the audit trail is not a logging convenience — it is a regulatory requirement and a legal artifact. Every agent action that touches a regulated process, a customer record, or a financial transaction must produce an immutable record. The 7-year retention period reflects regulatory minimums; some jurisdictions and instrument types require longer. This pattern enforces audit trail creation as a non-negotiable property of all agent actions in FS context, implementing the Accountability pillar of F17 (Responsible AI) at the code level.
trigger: Any agent action in an FS context. No exceptions.
logic: 1. Before any agent action completes: check that an audit log entry will be created. 2. Required log fields: timestamp (UTC, millisecond precision), user context (user ID or system process ID), action taken (structured description), data accessed (field list, NOT values for PII fields), outcome (success, partial, failed), authority level used, pattern ID that triggered the action, session ID. 3. Write log entry atomically with the action — if log write fails, action does not complete. 4. Log storage: append-only data store. No update or delete operations permitted on log entries. 5. Log retention: 7 years minimum from creation date. Automated retention enforcement. 6. Log access: read-only to authorized users. Any access to audit logs is itself logged.
escalation: If log write fails: action is blocked, incident logged to error queue, Compliance Officer notified. If any attempt to modify or delete log entries is detected: immediate security incident response, CISO notified.
ground_truth_check: Annual audit: are all required fields present in every log entry? Completeness rate must be 100%. Any missing entry is an audit finding. External auditor spot-check confirms log integrity.
promotion_criteria: Already at maturity 5 — enforced universally, no exceptions permitted. Annual compliance audit validates implementation. External auditor confirmation of log completeness and immutability.

name: PII Access Pattern
description: Customer PII — names, SSNs, account numbers, addresses — is the highest-sensitivity data category in financial services. Unnecessary access is a liability: it creates breach exposure, regulatory risk, and erodes customer trust. This pattern implements the Privacy pillar of F17 (Responsible AI) at the data access layer: agents access only the PII fields required for the specific task, log every access, and never include PII in outputs unless the task explicitly requires it. Need-to-know is not a preference; it is the access model.
trigger: Any agent query or data access operation that touches PII fields (defined as: customer name, SSN/TIN, date of birth, account number, routing number, home address, phone number, email address, government ID numbers).
logic: 1. Before data access: identify which fields the current task requires. Document task-to-field justification. 2. Request only the justified fields — do not pull full customer record when only account number is needed. 3. Log every PII access: timestamp, agent process, task ID, fields accessed (field names only, not values), justification. 4. In agent outputs: include PII only if the output's purpose requires it (e.g., a draft letter needs customer name; an internal risk analysis does not). For internal outputs: replace PII with tokens or partial values (last 4 digits of account number). 5. Never include PII in: pattern candidate logs, correction history, false-done logs, or any logs that flow to non-production systems. 6. If agent accesses PII outside its task justification: flag as access anomaly, log to security queue, notify Compliance Officer.
escalation: PII access anomaly: Compliance Officer + CISO (if pattern of anomalies detected). Customer data breach involving PII accessed by agent: immediate incident response per breach protocol.
ground_truth_check: Privacy audit: are agents accessing only the fields justified by their task? Are outputs correctly masking PII for internal use? Compliance Officer confirms pattern is functioning as designed.
promotion_criteria: Zero unauthorized PII access incidents attributable to pattern misconfiguration. Privacy audit passes with no material findings. 100+ tasks processed with correct PII handling. Compliance Officer sign-off.
